Title: Seedling growth and fall armyworm feeding preference influenced by dhurrin production in sorghum

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Mutation in dhurrinase2 reduces capacity to release HCN and is referenced as increasing susceptibility to insect feeding; in this paper it is used only in calibration/background context, not as a main NIL target.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SbEMS932 dhr2-1 was included in calibration experiments for imaging and feeding damage quantification. 2. The paper cites prior evidence that reduced HCN release due to dhurrinase2 mutation increases susceptibility to chewing insects. 3. This gene was not the primary focus of mechanistic testing in the main NIL greenhouse/field results.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Calibration comparisons; Feigl-Anger context from prior work
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: FAW infestation in greenhouse calibration studies
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Maintaining HCN release capacity may help preserve insect resistance, but this paper does not test deployment directly.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included here as a common metabolism gene because it affects defense output through hydrolysis/release rather than being the core experimentally tested pathway target in the main results.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: Tx623; SbEMS2447 mutant derived in Tx623 background
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Tx623; Tx623 bmr6; Tx623 bmr6 cyp79a1
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Committed entry step of cyanogenic glucoside biosynthesis from tyrosine
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes two N-hydroxylation reactions on tyrosine to form an unstable N,N-dihydroxytyrosine intermediate, followed by dehydration and decarboxylation to yield (E)-p-hydroxyphenylacetaldoxime, the first dedicated intermediate toward dhurrin.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Chemical defense against chewing insects through HCN release; also implicated in nitrogen economy and drought-related responses as discussed in the paper
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Seedling leaves, especially top collared leaf at 3-leaf stage
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies precursor pool; required for target-product biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss-of-function cyp79a1 abolishes dhurrin production/HCN release, increases seedling growth under non-infested conditions, and increases susceptibility/preference to fall armyworm feeding.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A C493Y mutation in CYP79A1 was used to generate near-isogenic sorghum lines lacking dhurrin and HCN release. 2. Dhurrin-free Tx623 bmr6 cyp79a1 seedlings showed higher green plant area and dry weight than wild type under non-infested conditions. 3. Under FAW infestation, cyp79a1 plants showed earlier and larger reductions in green plant area and dry weight, indicating greater feeding preference/susceptibility. 4. Field trials confirmed that Tx623 bmr6 cyp79a1 showed the clearest infestation-associated reductions in leaf area and dry weight. 5. The results reveal a metabolic tradeoff: dhurrin biosynthesis reduces growth potential but improves insect defense.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic mutant/NIL comparison; Feigl-Anger HCN assay; UHPLC dhurrin quantification; greenhouse and field insect feeding trials; nondestructive imaging; dry weight and leaf area phenotyping
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Seedling stage analyzed at 3- to 4-leaf stage
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native mutant allele in NILs; no transgenic promoter used
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fall armyworm infestation; greenhouse versus field conditions; seedling developmental stage
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key control/committed-step enzyme for dhurrin production
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Disruption improves vegetative growth in non-infested conditions but compromises insect resistance and may affect broader nitrogen-use and drought-response functions discussed by authors.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Plant growth and development datasets deposited at Purdue University Research Repository (PURR); no transcriptomics reported
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating CYP79A1/dhurrin biosynthesis could increase early growth and productivity where insect pressure is controlled, but reduced dhurrin may incur major herbivory risk; deployment should consider insect management and possible effects on nitrogen-use efficiency and drought tolerance.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper distinguishes this cyp79a1 C493Y allele from the previously described tcd1/P414L CYP79A1 allele, suggesting different structural/phenotypic consequences despite both eliminating dhurrin production.
